CY1.tagline: From number theory to provably secure cryptosystems and protocols.
CY1.subtitle: provable security, number-theoretic hardness, and the design of cryptographic protocols
foundations: group theory, finite fields, and modular arithmetic
computational hardness assumptions: factoring, discrete log, CDH and DDH
one-way functions and trapdoor permutations
IND-CPA and IND-CCA indistinguishability security games
reductionist security proofs and the random oracle model
existential unforgeability under chosen-message attack
lattice problems and post-quantum hardness

literature.title: New Directions in Cryptography
A Method for Obtaining Digital Signatures and Public-Key Cryptosystems
literature.authors: Ronald Rivest, Adi Shamir, Leonard Adleman

literature.title: Probabilistic Encryption
Random Oracles Are Practical: A Paradigm for Designing Efficient Protocols
FIPS 197: Advanced Encryption Standard (AES)
Curve25519: New Diffie-Hellman Speed Records

CY1.build: Built a rigorous applied-cryptography library in Python from big-integer arithmetic primitives up to authenticated key exchange, with AES-GCM AEAD, RSA-OAEP, ECDH, EdDSA, and a hybrid post-quantum KEM via liboqs, each increment backed by test vectors and a demonstrated attack on a naive variant.
objectives: Explain the algebraic foundations of cryptography: groups, rings, finite fields, and modular arithmetic.
State and apply the hardness assumptions underpinning modern crypto: factoring, discrete log, and CDH/DDH.
Formalize security definitions such as IND-CPA, IND-CCA, and existential unforgeability, and reason with reduction proofs.
Construct and analyze symmetric primitives: block ciphers, modes of operation, and authenticated encryption.
Build public-key schemes including RSA, ElGamal, and elliptic-curve cryptography from their mathematical structure.
Design hash functions, MACs, and digital signatures, and argue their collision and forgery resistance.
Implement authenticated key exchange and reason about PKI, certificates, and trust models.
Identify and exploit common protocol pitfalls: nonce reuse, padding oracles, and timing leaks.
Apply provable-security reasoning to evaluate whether a construction meets its claimed guarantees.
Evaluate post-quantum candidates and migration paths against classical schemes.
prerequisites: Discrete mathematics and basic number theory

weeks.title: Number theory and algebraic structures
weeks.lecture: Cover modular arithmetic, the Euclidean algorithm, groups, rings, finite fields, and Euler's theorem as the language of cryptography.
weeks.practice: Implement modular exponentiation, GCD, and modular inverse, and benchmark them on large integers.
weeks.project: Set up the crypto library skeleton and a tested big-integer modular arithmetic core.

weeks.title: Hardness assumptions and security definitions
weeks.lecture: Introduce one-way functions, the factoring and discrete-log problems, CDH/DDH, and formal indistinguishability-based security definitions.
weeks.practice: Build a discrete-log toy solver and measure how runtime scales to motivate the hardness assumption.
weeks.project: Add a primitives interface and a security-definition test harness to the library.

weeks.title: Block ciphers and modes of operation
weeks.lecture: Analyze pseudorandom permutations, the AES structure, and CBC, CTR, and GCM modes with their security properties.
weeks.practice: Wrap a vetted AES implementation, implement CTR and CBC modes, and write known-answer tests.
weeks.project: Integrate AES with multiple modes into the library with passing test vectors.

weeks.title: Authenticated encryption and MACs
weeks.lecture: Define IND-CCA security, present HMAC and GMAC, and explain encrypt-then-MAC and AEAD constructions.
weeks.practice: Implement HMAC and an encrypt-then-MAC AEAD wrapper, then attack a deliberately broken MAC-then-encrypt variant.
weeks.project: Add authenticated encryption with associated data and negative-test the broken variant.

weeks.title: Hash functions and the random oracle model
weeks.lecture: Cover collision, preimage, and second-preimage resistance, Merkle-Damgard and sponge constructions, and the random oracle heuristic.
weeks.practice: Team presentation: each team defends its project specification, threat model, and chosen security definitions before the class.
weeks.project: Freeze the project specification document approved at the milestone review.

weeks.title: RSA: construction and attacks
weeks.lecture: Derive RSA from Euler's theorem, prove correctness, and survey attacks: small exponents, common modulus, and padding oracles.
weeks.practice: Implement textbook RSA, then exploit it with a Bleichenbacher-style padding oracle on a vulnerable endpoint.
weeks.project: Add OAEP-padded RSA encryption and a regression test that the padding oracle is closed.

weeks.title: Discrete-log systems and ElGamal
weeks.lecture: Present cyclic groups, ElGamal encryption, the DDH assumption, and semantic security reductions.
weeks.practice: Implement ElGamal over a safe prime group and verify the homomorphic property experimentally.
weeks.project: Integrate ElGamal encryption with parameter validation into the library.

weeks.title: Elliptic-curve cryptography
weeks.lecture: Introduce the group law on elliptic curves, ECDH, and the elliptic-curve discrete-log problem and its efficiency advantages.
weeks.practice: Team presentation: interim demo of the working crypto library with a live encryption and decryption walkthrough.
weeks.project: Add an ECDH key-agreement module and present the interim build.

weeks.title: Digital signatures and unforgeability
weeks.lecture: Define existential unforgeability under chosen-message attack and analyze RSA-PSS, ECDSA, and EdDSA.
weeks.practice: Implement ECDSA signing and verification and demonstrate the catastrophic nonce-reuse key-recovery attack.
weeks.project: Add EdDSA signatures and a deterministic-nonce safeguard to the library.

weeks.title: Authenticated key exchange and PKI
weeks.lecture: Cover Diffie-Hellman, man-in-the-middle threats, certificates, chains of trust, and forward secrecy.
weeks.practice: Build an authenticated Diffie-Hellman handshake and a minimal certificate verifier with chain validation.
weeks.project: Add a handshake protocol and certificate-chain verification to the library.

weeks.title: Protocol design and common pitfalls
weeks.lecture: Analyze replay, reflection, downgrade, and timing attacks, and the principles of robust protocol design.
weeks.practice: Run a constant-time review, fix a timing leak in the project, and verify with statistical timing measurement.
weeks.project: Harden the protocol layer against replay and timing side channels.

weeks.title: Provable security and post-quantum cryptography
weeks.lecture: Walk through full reduction proofs and survey lattice-based and post-quantum standards and migration strategy.
weeks.practice: Integrate a post-quantum KEM via liboqs and design a hybrid classical-plus-PQ key exchange.
weeks.project: Add a hybrid post-quantum key-exchange option to the library.

weeks.title: Final integration and defense
weeks.lecture: Review the full cryptographic stack, security arguments, and threat coverage across the project.
weeks.practice: Team presentation: final demonstration with oral defense of design choices, security proofs, and known limitations.
weeks.project: Deliver the complete, tested cryptographic library with documentation and security rationale.

project.summary: Teams build a small but rigorous applied-cryptography library from arithmetic primitives up to authenticated protocols. Each increment is backed by a security argument, test vectors, and at least one demonstrated attack on a naive variant. The capstone is a coherent, documented library defended with provable-security reasoning.

tools: Python: implementation language for the teaching library
PyCryptodome: vetted primitives for comparison and wrapping
OpenSSL: reference implementation and certificate tooling
SageMath: number-theory and elliptic-curve experimentation
cryptography (pyca): modern high-level crypto APIs
liboqs: post-quantum KEM and signature implementations
Hashcat: hash-cracking to motivate strength requirements
dudect: constant-time leakage testing for timing attacks
GMP: high-performance big-integer arithmetic
pytest: test-vector and regression test harness

references.title: A Graduate Course in Applied Cryptography
Handbook of Applied Cryptography
references.authors: Alfred Menezes, Paul van Oorschot, Scott Vanstone

references.title: Cryptography Engineering: Design Principles and Practical Applications
references.authors: Niels Ferguson, Bruce Schneier, Tadayoshi Kohno

references.title: SP 800-56A Rev. 3: Pair-Wise Key-Establishment Using Discrete Logarithm Cryptography

CY2.tagline: Securing networks with protocols, perimeters, and attack detection.
CY2.subtitle: layered network defense, authenticated channels, and the theory of intrusion detection
foundations: the layered network stack and per-layer threat models
confidentiality, integrity, and availability as security goals
authenticated key exchange and forward secrecy
the public-key infrastructure trust model and chains of trust
signature-based versus anomaly-based detection theory
the security association and tunneling model of IPsec
defense in depth and zero-trust segmentation principles

literature.title: The Protection of Information in Computer Systems
Using Encryption for Authentication in Large Networks of Computers
Security Problems in the TCP/IP Protocol Suite
RFC 4301: Security Architecture for the Internet Protocol
RFC 8446: The Transport Layer Security (TLS) Protocol Version 1.3

CY2.build: Built a progressively defended virtual network lab in containers with a hardened TLS 1.3 endpoint, nftables and pfSense microsegmentation, Suricata and Zeek detection sensors, IPsec and WireGuard tunnels, and a Wazuh SIEM correlation pipeline, validated in a live red-versus-blue exercise.
objectives: Explain the layered security model and threats at each layer of the network stack.
Analyze the TLS 1.3 handshake, its cryptographic foundations, and its formal security goals.
Design secure protocol deployments using certificates, cipher suites, and forward secrecy.
Configure and reason about firewalls, network segmentation, and zero-trust boundaries.
Deploy and tune intrusion detection and prevention systems with signature and anomaly rules.
Build VPNs using IPsec and WireGuard and evaluate their trust and key-management models.
Capture and dissect traffic to detect reconnaissance, exfiltration, and protocol abuse.
Detect and explain common attacks: spoofing, MITM, downgrade, and denial of service.
Apply detection-engineering principles to write and validate effective alert rules.
Assess the security of a network architecture against a structured threat model.
prerequisites: Linux command line and basic scripting

weeks.title: Network threat models and the security stack
weeks.lecture: Review the OSI and TCP/IP stacks, attacker capabilities at each layer, and the goals of confidentiality, integrity, and availability.
weeks.practice: Build a lab network in containers or VMs and map its attack surface with reconnaissance tools.
weeks.project: Stand up the lab topology and document the initial threat model.

weeks.title: Transport security and TLS internals
weeks.lecture: Walk through the TLS 1.3 handshake, key schedule, AEAD record layer, and forward secrecy guarantees.
weeks.practice: Trace a real TLS 1.3 handshake byte by byte in Wireshark and identify each message.
weeks.project: Add an instrumented TLS endpoint to the lab and capture its handshakes.

weeks.title: Certificates, PKI, and protocol downgrade
weeks.lecture: Cover certificate validation, chains of trust, OCSP, and downgrade and stripping attacks on secure protocols.
weeks.practice: Run sslyze and testssl.sh against the endpoint and exploit a deliberately weakened configuration.
weeks.project: Harden the lab TLS endpoint and record before-and-after scan results.

weeks.title: Firewalls and network segmentation
weeks.lecture: Explain stateful filtering, default-deny policy, segmentation, and the zero-trust architecture model.
weeks.practice: Configure nftables and pfSense rules to segment the lab and verify enforcement with port scans.
weeks.project: Add firewall segmentation between the lab zones with a documented ruleset.

weeks.title: Designing the defended architecture
weeks.lecture: Discuss defense in depth, choke points, and how detection and prevention fit into network design.
weeks.practice: Team presentation: each team defends its network security specification and detection plan.
weeks.project: Freeze the network architecture and detection specification document.

weeks.title: Intrusion detection fundamentals
weeks.lecture: Compare signature-based and anomaly-based detection, and the IDS versus IPS placement trade-offs.
weeks.practice: Deploy Suricata in the lab and write a first signature to catch a known exploit pattern.
weeks.project: Add an IDS sensor to the lab with an initial rule set.

weeks.title: Traffic analysis and protocol forensics
weeks.lecture: Cover flow analysis, encrypted-traffic fingerprinting, and indicators of reconnaissance and exfiltration.
weeks.practice: Analyze a malicious pcap with Zeek and Wireshark to reconstruct an attack timeline.
weeks.project: Add a Zeek-based flow logging pipeline to the lab.

weeks.title: Detection engineering and tuning
weeks.lecture: Explain rule quality, false-positive management, and validation of detection coverage.
weeks.practice: Team presentation: interim demo of the lab detecting a live simulated attack end to end.
weeks.project: Demonstrate the interim detection pipeline against a scripted attack.

weeks.title: IPsec and VPN architecture
weeks.lecture: Present IKE, IPsec tunnels, the security associations model, and site-to-site versus remote-access designs.
weeks.practice: Build an IPsec tunnel between lab zones and inspect the negotiated parameters.
weeks.project: Add an encrypted IPsec tunnel connecting two lab segments.

weeks.title: Modern VPNs and key management
weeks.lecture: Compare WireGuard's cryptographic design with IPsec and discuss key distribution and rotation.
weeks.practice: Deploy WireGuard, then attempt and detect a key-compromise scenario in the lab.
weeks.project: Add a WireGuard overlay and a key-rotation procedure to the lab.

weeks.title: Active network attacks and mitigations
weeks.lecture: Analyze ARP and DNS spoofing, man-in-the-middle, and denial-of-service techniques and their defenses.
weeks.practice: Launch a controlled MITM and DoS against the lab and confirm the detection rules fire.
weeks.project: Validate that the detection layer catches active attacks and tune the rules.

weeks.title: Centralized monitoring and response
weeks.lecture: Cover log aggregation, SIEM correlation, alert triage, and incident escalation workflows.
weeks.practice: Ship lab logs into a SIEM, build correlation alerts, and run a triage exercise.
weeks.project: Integrate a SIEM dashboard and alerting into the lab.

weeks.title: Final architecture and red-team defense
weeks.lecture: Review the complete defended architecture and the detection coverage achieved across the term.
weeks.practice: Team presentation: final red-team-versus-blue-team demonstration with oral defense of the design.
weeks.project: Deliver the complete monitored, segmented, attack-resilient lab with documentation.

project.summary: Teams build and progressively defend a realistic virtual network, adding secure protocols, segmentation, VPNs, and a full detection and monitoring stack. Each week introduces an attack and a corresponding mitigation that must be demonstrated. The capstone is a live red-versus-blue exercise defended orally.

tools: Wireshark: packet capture and protocol dissection
Suricata: signature and anomaly intrusion detection
Snort: alternative IDS/IPS rule engine
Zeek: network flow analysis and security logging
nftables: stateful Linux firewall configuration
pfSense: firewall and routing appliance for the lab
WireGuard: modern VPN tunneling
strongSwan: IPsec and IKE VPN implementation
testssl.sh: TLS configuration auditing
Wazuh: SIEM, log correlation, and alerting

references.title: RFC 8446: The Transport Layer Security (TLS) Protocol Version 1.3
Cryptography and Network Security: Principles and Practice, 8th Edition
Wireshark User's Guide
references.authors: Richard Sharpe, Ulf Lamping, et al.

references.title: SP 800-77 Rev. 1: Guide to IPsec VPNs
The Illustrated TLS 1.3 Connection: Every Byte Explained

CY3.tagline: Attacking and defending hardware, firmware, and embedded devices.
CY3.subtitle: physical attacker models, side-channel leakage, and hardware roots of trust
foundations: the physical attacker model and embedded attack surface
the leakage model behind power, timing, and electromagnetic side channels
differential power analysis and its hypothesis-testing statistics
fault models for voltage, clock, and electromagnetic glitching
measured and verified boot and rollback protection
hardware roots of trust and the TPM 2.0 sealed-storage model
masking, hiding, and constant-time countermeasure theory

literature.title: Timing Attacks on Implementations of Diffie-Hellman, RSA, DSS, and Other Systems
Tamper Resistance: a Cautionary Note
On the Importance of Checking Cryptographic Protocols for Faults
literature.authors: Dan Boneh, Richard DeMillo, Richard Lipton

literature.title: Differential Power Analysis
literature.authors: Paul Kocher, Joshua Jaffe, Benjamin Jun

literature.title: TPM 2.0 Library Specification

CY3.build: Performed a full hardware security assessment of an embedded device, mapping UART and SPI interfaces, dumping and reverse-engineering firmware with binwalk and Ghidra, extracting an AES key by differential power analysis on ChipWhisperer, then adding masking, constant-time fixes, signed updates, and debug-port lockdown.
objectives: Explain embedded threat models and the expanded attack surface of physical devices.
Describe the theory of side channels: power, timing, and electromagnetic leakage.
Mount differential power analysis and reason about its statistical foundations.
Analyze fault-injection and glitching attacks against secure boot and key handling.
Explain secure boot, hardware roots of trust, and the TPM 2.0 architecture.
Extract and reverse engineer firmware to recover secrets and logic.
Identify hardware interfaces such as UART, JTAG, and SPI and use them for analysis.
Design countermeasures: masking, constant-time code, and tamper resistance.
Assess an embedded product against a structured hardware threat model.
Evaluate the trade-offs between security, cost, and performance in hardware design.

weeks.title: Embedded threat models and attack surface
weeks.lecture: Survey embedded system architectures, the physical attacker model, and the spectrum of hardware attacks.
weeks.practice: Tear down a target board, identify components and interfaces, and document its attack surface.
weeks.project: Choose the target device and produce its initial hardware threat model.

weeks.title: Debug interfaces and bus sniffing
weeks.lecture: Explain UART, JTAG, SWD, SPI, and I2C and how exposed debug ports become entry points.
weeks.practice: Locate and connect to a UART console and sniff an SPI bus with a logic analyzer.
weeks.project: Map and access the debug and bus interfaces on the target.

weeks.title: Firmware extraction and analysis
weeks.lecture: Cover flash dumping, firmware image formats, and static reverse engineering of embedded binaries.
weeks.practice: Dump flash over SPI, unpack the firmware with binwalk, and disassemble it in Ghidra.
weeks.project: Extract the target firmware and begin a reverse-engineering map.

weeks.title: Finding secrets and logic flaws
weeks.lecture: Discuss hardcoded keys, weak update mechanisms, and vulnerability patterns in firmware.
weeks.practice: Hunt for hardcoded credentials and an update-verification flaw in the dumped firmware.
weeks.project: Document recovered secrets and a firmware vulnerability in the target.

weeks.title: Threat model and analysis specification
weeks.lecture: Review how interface, firmware, and physical findings combine into a coherent attack plan.
weeks.practice: Team presentation: each team defends its target, threat model, and planned attack chain.
weeks.project: Freeze the project specification and attack-plan document.

weeks.title: Side-channel theory and timing attacks
weeks.lecture: Introduce the leakage model, timing attacks on cryptographic implementations, and the underlying statistics.
weeks.practice: Reproduce a timing attack to recover key bits from a vulnerable software routine.
weeks.project: Add a timing-leak analysis of the target's crypto operations.

weeks.title: Power analysis and DPA
weeks.lecture: Derive simple and differential power analysis and the hypothesis-testing model behind key recovery.
weeks.practice: Capture power traces on ChipWhisperer and run DPA to extract an AES key.
weeks.project: Mount a power-analysis attack against an encryption routine on the target.

weeks.title: Fault injection and glitching
weeks.lecture: Explain voltage and clock glitching, fault models, and bypassing security checks via faults.
weeks.practice: Team presentation: interim demo of a working side-channel or fault attack on the target.
weeks.project: Demonstrate the interim physical attack against the device.

weeks.title: Secure boot and chain of trust
weeks.lecture: Cover measured and verified boot, signature verification, and rollback protection in the boot chain.
weeks.practice: Analyze a secure-boot implementation and attempt a glitch to bypass signature verification.
weeks.project: Evaluate and attack the target's boot integrity mechanism.

weeks.title: TPM and hardware key storage
weeks.lecture: Present the TPM 2.0 architecture, sealed storage, attestation, and hardware-backed key isolation.
weeks.practice: Use a TPM to seal a secret and reason about which attacks it does and does not stop.
weeks.project: Design a TPM-backed key-protection improvement for the target.

weeks.title: Countermeasures and constant-time design
weeks.lecture: Discuss masking, hiding, constant-time coding, and tamper-resistant design principles.
weeks.practice: Add masking and constant-time fixes to a routine and re-run the side-channel attack to measure improvement.
weeks.project: Implement and validate a countermeasure on the target.

weeks.title: Hardened firmware and secure updates
weeks.lecture: Cover signed updates, anti-rollback, debug-port lockdown, and secure provisioning.
weeks.practice: Implement a signed-update verifier and lock down the exposed debug interfaces.
weeks.project: Harden the firmware update path and disable insecure interfaces.

weeks.title: Final assessment and defense
weeks.lecture: Review the full attack chain and the defenses applied across the term.
weeks.practice: Team presentation: final demonstration of attacks and mitigations with oral defense of findings.
weeks.project: Deliver the complete hardware security assessment with attacks, fixes, and report.

project.summary: Teams perform a full hardware security assessment of a real embedded device, progressing from interface mapping and firmware extraction to side-channel and fault attacks, then to designed countermeasures. Each finding is reproduced and documented. The capstone presents both the demonstrated attacks and the hardened result with an oral defense.

tools: ChipWhisperer: power-analysis and fault-injection platform
Ghidra: firmware disassembly and reverse engineering
binwalk: firmware extraction and analysis
Saleae Logic: logic analyzer for bus sniffing
flashrom: SPI flash dumping and writing
OpenOCD: JTAG and SWD debugging
QEMU: firmware emulation for dynamic analysis
Bus Pirate: low-level bus interfacing
tpm2-tools: TPM 2.0 experimentation and sealing
radare2: binary analysis and patching

references.title: Power Analysis Attacks: Revealing the Secrets of Smart Cards
references.authors: Stefan Mangard, Elisabeth Oswald, Thomas Popp

references.title: Practical IoT Hacking
references.authors: Fotios Chantzis, Ioannis Stais, Paulino Calderon, Evangelos Deirmentzoglou, Beau Woods

references.title: Timing Attacks on Implementations of Diffie-Hellman, RSA, DSS, and Other Systems

CY4.tagline: Managing cyber-security through risk, frameworks, audit, and response.
CY4.subtitle: structured risk, control frameworks, auditable evidence, and incident governance
foundations: governance, accountability, and the alignment of security with business goals
the threat, vulnerability, likelihood, and impact risk model
qualitative and quantitative risk-assessment methods
the risk management framework lifecycle and control selection
the management-system model and the plan-do-check-act cycle
control catalogs, tailoring, and traceability to evidence
the incident-handling lifecycle and continuous monitoring

literature.title: SP 800-30 Rev. 1: Guide for Conducting Risk Assessments
SP 800-37 Rev. 2: Risk Management Framework for Information Systems and Organizations
SP 800-53 Rev. 5: Security and Privacy Controls for Information Systems and Organizations
ISO/IEC 27001:2022: Information Security Management Systems, Requirements
CSWP 29: The NIST Cybersecurity Framework (CSF) 2.0

CY4.build: Authored a complete security governance program for a case-study organization, producing a prioritized SP 800-30 risk register, a CSF 2.0 profile, an ISO/IEC 27001 ISMS with statement of applicability, tailored SP 800-53 controls, an auditable policy suite, a mock internal audit, and a tabletop-validated incident response plan.
objectives: Explain governance, risk, and compliance and how they align security with business goals.
Conduct structured risk assessments using qualitative and quantitative methods.
Apply the NIST Risk Management Framework and the Cybersecurity Framework 2.0.
Build an information security management system aligned to ISO/IEC 27001.
Select and document security controls from established control catalogs.
Draft security policies, standards, and procedures that are auditable and enforceable.
Plan and execute audits and assess control effectiveness against evidence.
Design an incident response capability and run it through realistic exercises.
Evaluate compliance posture against regulatory and contractual obligations.
Communicate risk and security decisions effectively to executive stakeholders.
prerequisites: Basic understanding of IT systems and operations

weeks.title: Governance, risk, and compliance overview
weeks.lecture: Define GRC, the roles of governance and accountability, and how security supports organizational objectives.
weeks.practice: Profile a case-study organization and map its assets, stakeholders, and obligations.
weeks.project: Select the organization and produce its context and scope document.

weeks.title: Risk concepts and assessment methods
weeks.lecture: Cover threats, vulnerabilities, likelihood, impact, and qualitative versus quantitative risk methods.
weeks.practice: Build an asset inventory and a first threat-and-vulnerability list for the organization.
weeks.project: Add an asset register and threat catalog to the project.

weeks.title: Conducting a risk assessment
weeks.lecture: Present the SP 800-30 risk assessment process and risk-rating and prioritization techniques.
weeks.practice: Run a structured risk assessment and produce a ranked risk register.
weeks.project: Add a complete prioritized risk register to the project.

weeks.title: Risk management frameworks
weeks.lecture: Explain the NIST RMF lifecycle and the Cybersecurity Framework 2.0 functions and outcomes.
weeks.practice: Map the organization's risks and current state onto the CSF 2.0 functions.
weeks.project: Add a CSF 2.0 current-state profile to the project.

weeks.title: Program specification and scope
weeks.lecture: Discuss how risk results drive control selection and the structure of a security program.
weeks.practice: Team presentation: each team defends its risk assessment and proposed program scope.
weeks.project: Freeze the security program specification and scope.

weeks.title: ISO/IEC 27001 and the ISMS
weeks.lecture: Present the ISO/IEC 27001 management-system model, the PDCA cycle, and the statement of applicability.
weeks.practice: Draft a statement of applicability and ISMS scope for the organization.
weeks.project: Add the ISMS scope and statement of applicability to the project.

weeks.title: Control selection and the control catalog
weeks.lecture: Cover control families, the SP 800-53 catalog, and tailoring controls to assessed risk.
weeks.practice: Select and tailor controls to treat the top risks in the register.
weeks.project: Add a tailored control set mapped to the risk register.

weeks.title: Policies, standards, and procedures
weeks.lecture: Explain the policy hierarchy, enforceability, and writing controls that can be audited.
weeks.practice: Team presentation: interim review of the ISMS, controls, and draft policy set.
weeks.project: Present the interim program with its initial policy suite.

weeks.title: Authoring an auditable policy suite
weeks.lecture: Discuss roles and responsibilities, exceptions, and mapping policies to controls and evidence.
weeks.practice: Write a full policy and matching procedure for a chosen control domain.
weeks.project: Add a complete policy and procedure set to the program.

weeks.title: Audit and control assessment
weeks.lecture: Cover audit planning, evidence gathering, sampling, and assessing control effectiveness.
weeks.practice: Conduct a mock internal audit and document findings and nonconformities.
weeks.project: Add an internal audit report with findings to the project.

weeks.title: Remediation and continuous improvement
weeks.lecture: Explain corrective action plans, risk acceptance, and continuous monitoring.
weeks.practice: Build a remediation plan and a continuous-monitoring approach for open findings.
weeks.project: Add a corrective-action and monitoring plan to the program.

weeks.title: Incident response and resilience
weeks.lecture: Present the SP 800-61 incident-handling lifecycle, roles, and communication and escalation paths.
weeks.practice: Run a tabletop incident exercise and capture lessons learned for the plan.
weeks.project: Add an incident response plan validated by a tabletop exercise.

weeks.title: Final program and defense
weeks.lecture: Review the complete GRC program and how its parts trace from risk to control to evidence.
weeks.practice: Team presentation: final program delivery with oral defense to a simulated steering committee.
weeks.project: Deliver the complete GRC program package with documentation and defense.

project.summary: Teams build a complete security governance program for a case-study organization, progressing from risk assessment through framework alignment, control selection, policy authoring, audit, and incident response. Each deliverable traces from assessed risk to control to auditable evidence. The capstone defends the full program before a simulated steering committee.

tools: Eramba: GRC and risk-management platform
OpenSCAP: automated control compliance scanning
Wazuh: continuous monitoring and audit evidence
NIST CPRT: control and framework reference toolkit
MITRE ATT&CK Navigator: threat-informed control mapping
FAIR-U: quantitative risk-analysis training tool
draw.io: architecture and data-flow diagramming
Git: version control for policies and evidence
GRC spreadsheet templates: risk registers and SoA tracking
TheHive: incident case management and response

references.title: SP 800-37 Rev. 2: Risk Management Framework for Information Systems and Organizations
SP 800-30 Rev. 1: Guide for Conducting Risk Assessments
SP 800-61 Rev. 2: Computer Security Incident Handling Guide
SP 800-53 Rev. 5: Security and Privacy Controls for Information Systems and Organizations

CY5.tagline: Building and securing blockchains, smart contracts, and DeFi.
CY5.subtitle: consensus under adversarial failure, smart-contract security, and decentralized trust
foundations: replication, the CAP trade-off, and distributed failure models
the FLP impossibility result for asynchronous consensus
Byzantine fault tolerance and the Byzantine generals problem
Nakamoto consensus, proof of work, and the longest-chain rule
hashes, digital signatures, and Merkle trees as integrity backbone
the smart-contract execution and gas-metering model
economic security assumptions and attack vectors of DeFi

literature.title: The Byzantine Generals Problem
literature.authors: Leslie Lamport, Robert Shostak, Marshall Pease

literature.title: Impossibility of Distributed Consensus with One Faulty Process
literature.authors: Michael Fischer, Nancy Lynch, Michael Paterson

literature.title: Practical Byzantine Fault Tolerance
Bitcoin: A Peer-to-Peer Electronic Cash System
The Bitcoin Backbone Protocol: Analysis and Applications
literature.authors: Juan Garay, Aggelos Kiayias, Nikos Leonardos

CY5.build: Built a minimal but complete decentralized system, layering a simplified PBFT agreement module, Merkle-backed block integrity, proof-of-work mining, deployed Solidity contracts, a hierarchical deterministic multi-signature wallet, and an automated market maker, then audited and hardened it against reentrancy and flash-loan attacks.
objectives: Explain the distributed-systems foundations: replication, the CAP trade-off, and failure models.
Analyze Byzantine fault tolerance and the theory behind consensus protocols.
Describe Nakamoto consensus and proof-of-work and proof-of-stake security arguments.
Apply the cryptographic foundations of blockchains: hashes, signatures, and Merkle trees.
Build and deploy smart contracts and reason about their execution model.
Identify and exploit smart-contract vulnerabilities such as reentrancy and integer flaws.
Design secure wallet and key-management systems including hierarchical deterministic keys.
Assess DeFi protocols for economic and technical attack vectors.
Evaluate the security and decentralization trade-offs of a system design.
Audit a smart contract and produce a structured findings report.
prerequisites: Programming experience in JavaScript or Python

weeks.title: Distributed systems and consensus theory
weeks.lecture: Cover replication, the CAP trade-off, failure models, and the FLP impossibility result that frames consensus.
weeks.practice: Simulate a small replicated log and observe how message loss and ordering break consistency.
weeks.project: Set up the project repo and a baseline distributed-ledger simulation.

weeks.title: Byzantine fault tolerance
weeks.lecture: Present the Byzantine generals problem and the PBFT protocol with its safety and liveness guarantees.
weeks.practice: Implement a simplified PBFT round and test it against simulated Byzantine nodes.
weeks.project: Add a Byzantine-fault-tolerant agreement module to the ledger.

weeks.title: Hashes, signatures, and Merkle trees
weeks.lecture: Review cryptographic hashing, digital signatures, and Merkle trees as the integrity backbone of blockchains.
weeks.practice: Build a Merkle tree and signature-verification layer for the ledger's blocks.
weeks.project: Add cryptographic block integrity and signed transactions to the ledger.

weeks.title: Nakamoto consensus and proof of work
weeks.lecture: Derive proof-of-work, the longest-chain rule, and the security analysis of the Bitcoin backbone protocol.
weeks.practice: Implement a proof-of-work miner and study fork resolution under competing chains.
weeks.project: Add proof-of-work mining and longest-chain selection to the ledger.

weeks.title: Design specification and threat model
weeks.lecture: Discuss proof-of-stake, finality, and the security and decentralization trade-offs across consensus designs.
weeks.practice: Team presentation: each team defends its system specification, consensus choice, and threat model.
weeks.project: Freeze the system specification and threat-model document.

weeks.title: Smart contracts and the EVM
weeks.lecture: Explain account models, the Ethereum Virtual Machine, gas, and the smart-contract execution lifecycle.
weeks.practice: Write, test, and deploy a first Solidity contract to a local development chain.
weeks.project: Add a deployed smart-contract layer to the project.

weeks.title: Smart-contract vulnerabilities
weeks.lecture: Analyze reentrancy, integer issues, access-control flaws, and oracle manipulation with real case studies.
weeks.practice: Exploit a vulnerable contract in a sandbox and then patch the reentrancy flaw.
weeks.project: Document and fix vulnerabilities in the project's contracts.

weeks.title: Secure development and testing
weeks.lecture: Cover secure patterns, invariants, formal-style testing, and the role of audits.
weeks.practice: Team presentation: interim demo of the deployed contracts with a live vulnerability and its fix.
weeks.project: Present the interim contract suite with a security test report.

weeks.title: Wallets and key management
weeks.lecture: Present elliptic-curve keys, address derivation, hierarchical deterministic wallets, and seed-phrase security.
weeks.practice: Build an HD wallet that derives keys and signs transactions for the ledger.
weeks.project: Add a hierarchical deterministic wallet to the project.

weeks.title: Custody and multi-signature security
weeks.lecture: Discuss hot versus cold storage, multi-signature schemes, threshold signatures, and key-recovery design.
weeks.practice: Implement a multi-signature approval flow and test recovery and revocation scenarios.
weeks.project: Add multi-signature custody controls to the wallet.

weeks.title: DeFi protocols and mechanisms
weeks.lecture: Explain automated market makers, lending, liquidity, and the economic assumptions DeFi relies on.
weeks.practice: Deploy a simple automated-market-maker contract and trace swaps and liquidity events.
weeks.project: Add a basic DeFi mechanism to the project's contract layer.

weeks.title: DeFi attacks and auditing
weeks.lecture: Cover flash-loan attacks, oracle manipulation, and economic exploits, plus the audit methodology.
weeks.practice: Run a flash-loan-style attack against the AMM, then audit and harden it.
weeks.project: Audit the DeFi layer and apply hardening fixes.

weeks.title: Final system and audit defense
weeks.lecture: Review the full stack from consensus through contracts and wallets and the security arguments at each layer.
weeks.practice: Team presentation: final demonstration with an oral defense of the audit report and design choices.
weeks.project: Deliver the complete decentralized system with a security audit report.

project.summary: Teams build a minimal but complete decentralized system, layering consensus, cryptographic integrity, smart contracts, wallets, and a DeFi mechanism, then audit and harden it. Each layer is accompanied by a demonstrated attack and a fix. The capstone defends a full security audit report orally.

tools: Solidity: smart-contract programming language
Foundry: contract development, testing, and fuzzing
Hardhat: Ethereum development and deployment environment
Remix: browser-based contract IDE and debugger
Slither: static analysis for Solidity vulnerabilities
Echidna: property-based smart-contract fuzzing
Ganache: local Ethereum test chain
ethers.js: blockchain interaction and wallet library
MetaMask: wallet and key-management client
Tenderly: transaction simulation and debugging

references.title: Mastering Bitcoin, 3rd Edition
references.authors: Andreas M. Antonopoulos, David A. Harding

references.title: Bitcoin: A Peer-to-Peer Electronic Cash System
Ethereum: A Next-Generation Smart Contract and Decentralized Application Platform
The Bitcoin Backbone Protocol: Analysis and Applications
references.authors: Juan Garay, Aggelos Kiayias, Nikos Leonardos

references.title: Bitcoin and Cryptocurrency Technologies
references.authors: Arvind Narayanan, Joseph Bonneau, Edward Felten, Andrew Miller, Steven Goldfeder
